data.title: MDY: A Primer On The Oldest Mid-Cap ETF
data.description:
Introduction to the State Street SPDR S&P MIDCAP 400 ETF Trust

The State Street SPDR S&P MIDCAP 400 ETF Trust (MDY), which is backed by State Street Corporation, has amassed total assets under management of $25 billion since its inception in April 1995. MDY pays dividends four times in a year, annualizing to a little over 1% at the current price. It has an expense ratio of 0.23%

What Does MDY Do?

MDY, which has been around for over three decades now at the time of writing, is considered to be the oldest US mid-cap ETF in this industry, and it fulfills its objective by mirroring the performance of The S&P MidCap 400® Index [SM4I], which is managed by the index provider S&P Dow Jones Indices LLC.

Just like how the S&P 500 is supposed to represent the 500 largest companies within the US, SM4I is designed to represent 400 mid-sized companies that have cleared certain profitability (prospective stocks should have delivered positive reported earnings over the most recent quarter, as well as the aggregate of the four more recent quarters), liquidity (at least 250K worth of shares of the stock should have been exchanged in each of the most recent 6 months before evaluation, as well as an annual dollar value traded to float adjusted market-cap ratio of 0.75x and above, and market-cap (unadjusted market-capitalization range of $8-$22.7 billion, with a float adjusted market-cap that amounts to at least 50% of the unadjusted range) thresholds.

Investors should also note that the holdings of SM4I don’t overlap with that of the S&P 500 or the S&P SmallCap 600 Index, for that matter.

MDY’s goal of mirroring SM4I is fulfilled by its trustee (as part of the 0.23% expense ratio that MDY charges investors, note that 0.1% consists of fees that go to its trustee), the Bank of New York Mellon Corporation (BK), which purchases all the same 400 stocks from the index, weighing them in the same proportion (the trustee also makes changes to the portfolio as and when the index makes changes, even if it is on a monthly basis).

What Are the Standout Traits of MDY’s Portfolio?

Even though MDY's tracking index weighs its individual stocks on the basis of their free-float-adjusted market capitalization, we don’t see a great deal of top-heaviness, which is what one usually sees with the S&P 500. To elaborate, the top 10 of MDY only account for 7% of the portfolio; in contrast, the top 10 of the S&P 500 have a more pronounced weight of 38%.

The reason for this is because the differential between the largest and smallest stocks in the S&P 500 is quite wide (a differential of almost 800x in their respective market caps). Whereas in the case of SM4I, the differential between the largest and smallest stocks is only around 20x. Secondly, investors should also consider that if a stock from SM41 becomes too large or too small (by way of market cap), it automatically slips into either the S&P 500 or the S&P Small Cap 600, so constituents here are typically within quite a narrow bracket.

With regards to sector exposure, MDY is well spread out amongst 11 different sectors, but still, a little over one-fourth of the portfolio consists of industrial stocks alone.

From a market-cap breakup, even though the average market cap of this portfolio of around $10 billion is in line with the mid-cap threshold, note that a large proportion of the holdings (over 64%) consists of small caps.

Stylistically, the biggest tilt (43%) goes towards hybrid stocks (stocks with both growth and value characteristics such as strong sales and earnings growth, high payouts, low price-to-earnings multiples, etc.)

What Are the Risks Associated With MDY?

Since MDY was established way back in 1995, it was set up under the archaic structure of a unit investment trust (UIT), rather than an open-ended fund. The drawback of UITs is that they are legally prohibited from using any dividend cash they receive from its 400 holdings to buy more stock during a quarter. Rather, these dividends need to be parked in a non-interest-bearing account until the end of the quarter. This prompts something of a cash drag during bull markets and when MDY’s holdings are rising.

We’ll elaborate on this in a while (in the peer section of the article), but MDY’s expense ratio of 0.23% doesn’t make it a very cost-efficient option for those who want to play the US mid-cap equity wave.

MDY’s annualized volatility profile is around 430 bps more than the standard ETF, which suggests that the returns it typically generates over a year tend to vary a great deal and make it difficult for someone who’s resorting to long-term planning.

MDY also has a very unappealing profile; its dividend growth has lagged the pace of a median ETF across different durations, and the dividend it currently offers of just a little over 1% is not even half as good as what a standard ETF offers. Some of this is also a function of its target cohort, the midcaps, who are still reinvesting a large chunk of their excess cash back into the business (rather than sharing it with their shareholders).

Who Is MDY For?

Mid-caps are seen as a well-balanced class that captures the best of both the small-cap world as well as the large-cap world while also offsetting some of the risks associated with those latter terrains. For instance, mid-caps are generally further down in their overall lifecycle, with more mature business models, and don’t necessarily struggle with the capital-raising challenges that small caps typically face. They are also not as volatile as small caps. Unlike large caps that are also largely close to hitting their maturity stage, mid-caps also offer better growth potential and at cheaper valuations. In fact, the valuation-earnings trade-off that these mid-caps offer is better than what small caps or large caps offer.

Note that mid-caps of MDY offer the best long-term earnings growth profile (11.2%) out of the three cohorts, yet can be picked up at a valuation multiple that is 25% cheaper than large caps.

MDY would also appeal to those who like ETFs whose holdings are well spread out and not clustered around the largest names (as noted earlier, MDY’s top 10 stocks account for a very low stake of around 7%). This opens up a window to profit from the movement of a wide group of stocks, rather than relying on just a handful of them.

MDY would also appeal to investors who appreciate ETFs that genuinely track the indices they claim to track in their marketing material (most passive ETFs only seek to publicize this facet in their marketing literature but eventually fail to replicate the performance of their respective indices). MDY’s exemplary tracking is illustrated by the low tracking errors across different timeframes (one, three, or five years).

For a product that also claims to replicate its index, note that it doesn’t see considerable churn of its holdings, and this reflects the SM4I. Basically, note that while most ETFs incur annual turnover ratios of 30%, MDY’s annual churn is roughly half of it and suggests that it would appeal to those investors who like to see some stability in their long-term holdings (lower turnover also means lower transaction costs, which would otherwise leave an inimical mark on the returns).

MDY's Peers

MDY may be the oldest, but it certainly isn’t the only ETF that passively tracks the S&P MidCap 400 Index. We present two younger and cheaper alternatives, in the shape of iShares Core S&P Mid-Cap ETF (IJH) and State Street SPDR Portfolio S&P 400 Mid Cap ETF (SPMD). SPMD is also issued by the same fund family—State Street—a decade later. What makes it different is its structure as the more flexible open-ended fund, which is how most ETFs are structured today. This enables it to avoid the cash drag that a UIT like MDY faces. It is also substantially cheaper than MDY at an expense ratio of just 0.03%. Though SPMD distributes on a quarterly basis like the other two, it has grown its dividends at a quicker pace and also offers a superior yield. SPMD also has the lowest degree of annual churn.

IJH, which is backed by BlackRock, Inc. (BLK), stands out for its AUM of over $111 billion and daily dollar volumes of nearly $1 billion a year and its very tight spreads. Relative to their respective AUMs, MDY sees the most daily volumes, which suggests that it is heavily pursued by those who like to make shorter-term investments.

Summary

MDY, the oldest ETF in the US mid-cap space, offers the best of both the small-cap and large-cap worlds, a tilt towards blended stocks, and a pretty compelling valuations-earnings tradeoff. However, MDY’s positioning as a trust is not ideal compared to other alternatives.

This article answers three main questions about MDY:

  1. What are the main features of MDY's portfolio?
  2. What type of investor is MDY suitable for, and what are the risks associated with it?
  3. How does MDY compare to other products that passively track the S&P MidCap 400 Index?

Editor's note: This article is intended to provide a general overview of the ETF for educational purposes only and, unlike other articles on Seeking Alpha, does not offer an investment opinion about the ETF.

data.title: Armada Hoffler's Portfolio Overhaul: What The 2026 Transition Means For Investors
data.description:
Introduction

As an analyst, I usually start my articles by going over the most recent quarterly results. This time I won't be going over the Q4 2025 results. Why? Effective March 2, Armada Hoffler Properties (AHH) will be rebranded to 'AH Realty Trust'. This will be a pretty meaningful repositioning of the REIT and we have to dive into the details to see whether this strategic evolution makes a lot of sense (or not). I will focus on the projected 2026 operational performance and implications of the decisions that have been made.

Let me explain what happened and what it means for investors.

From complexity to clarity: A more focused and predictable REIT

According to management, the renaming and rebranding of 'Armada Hoffler Properties' to 'AH Realty Trust' aligns with the company's evolution as a firm. In the latest Q4 2025 conference call, management said the following:

As you can see, management is planning to simplify its business model by divesting its multifamily portfolio and selling its construction management business as well as their real estate financing business. I already wrote about AHH's new management's focus on simplifying and deleveraging the company

Management is planning to use the sale-proceeds to pay down debt, redeploy capital towards retail assets and (perhaps) buy back shares.

There is a lot of demand for these assets/business segments that management is currently in the process of selling. It is important to understand that most of this is happening at cap rates around + 5%.

We are under an LOI for 11 of our 14 multifamily assets with a global real estate investment and management firm following a disciplined and targeted marketing process that began months ago, drawing strong interest from multiple credible parties...

… Despite the high quality of our assets, public market valuations do not reflect the underlying private market value of the portfolio. Exiting the multifamily portfolio unlocks significant embedded value by harvesting the arbitrage between public and private market valuations and accelerates our deleveraging program. In addition, the exit of our construction business is effectively complete, and we are substantially finalized on terms with the buyer.

Lastly, we have executed an LOI with an institutional buyer to acquire the interest in 2 of our 4 real estate financing investments, and we are in discussions with our partner to exit a third. The remaining investment is currently in the market with comparable cap rates in the low 5's.

Mr. market seems worried about AHH's sale of its low cap-rate and valuable multifamily business and I get it why this is the case. Office and Retail assets are perceived as less valuable and therefore trade at higher cap rates. But it is important to look at this from multiple angles and prevent yourself from exclusively focusing on the selling of great assets. The management of Healthpeak Properties (DOC) is doing the same thing right now. They are selling high-value 'outpatient medical' assets at good cap rates to buy into lab buildings in a post-COVID oversupplied market. Capitalizing on a (in their view) once-in-a-life-time opportunity. Or to quote DOC's management directly:

There's a big opportunity set there, and it's an awfully good time to invest in our view. But again, at the right price and the right submarket.

The point is this: do you want a REIT's management to sell assets when cap rates are high or when cap rates are low? If management knows their industry well and can anticipate on future industry trends (like the lack of office supply on which I will - again - touch at the end of this article), selling high and buying low makes a lot of sense. And this is what AHH's management is planning to do, among other things.

The most important question for dividend investors is: what will happen to the dividend, considering the fact that a lot of income-producing assets are about to get sold right now? AHH's management provides some guidance regarding this point, but this is the part that won't satisfy all investors.

Post transition FFO and dividend safety

Numbers can easily manipulate you, both in a positive and negative way. The same goes for AHH's 2026 FFO guidance. You can look at the pro forma 2026 FFO of $0.52 (at the midpoint) in the 2026 guidance presentation (page 2). The assumptions baked in this guidance are on page 3. When you use this FFO number as a valuation "anchor", you will understate the earnings power of the post-transformation portfolio. That is why I will go (and show) you the apples-to-apples comparison using the 2025 and post transformation 2026 FFO numbers.

As you can see, FFO will drop because the company is getting rid of the 'General Contracting & Real Estate Services' ($0.05). Selling this business segment reduces earnings volatility and helps the REIT focus on recurring and predictable rental income. The same logic applies to the real estate financing segment ($0.07). You are dealing with interest rate sensitivity, repayments timing, credit risk etc. It is a fine business (proven by the low cap rates), but it makes it a lot harder for Mr. Market to value Armada Hoffler accordingly. If the market doesn't award Armada with a higher P/FFO multiple because of its complex business structure, it makes sense to simplify it. REITs use share issuances accompanied with (preferably cheap) debt to fund future acquisitions and, because of this, grow FFO per share. Together with the sale of the multifamily portfolio ($0.13), Nareit FFO will drop by as much as $0.25. The mitigating factor will be coming from the interest savings and acquisitions (+ $0.11) that will mostly come from the big deleveraging that will happen once the sales are completed. You will arrive at a net decrease in 2026 Nareit FFO of $0.14 (or 18%) when compared to 2025.

Do you prefer volatile earnings to cover your dividend payments, or do you prefer a predictable (and slowly growing) dividend coverage? A superficial analysis will point out that AHH's management is not doing investors a favor, since FFO is dropping pretty dramatically! But you have to understand that the 2026 guidance does not tell you the entire story.

Let me give you a couple of examples to illustrate what I mean.

Deleveraging helps a REIT a lot. Not only does it attract more investors that are risk-averse and skip on highly leveraged REITs. It makes sense to prefer long-term (unsecured) debt at a fixed rate, rather than relying on complex derivative products.

And please don't forget that REITs are leveraged vehicles that need to refinance a portion of their debt every year (if the debt is laddered well). Deleveraging the entire company means that - going forward - there is the ability to refinance all future debt at relatively lower rates. The only situation in which I won't like a company selling great assets like AHH does is when it is being done in a bear market at fire sale prices. But just like the share issuance that was done back in September last year at $10.50 a share, management seems to be pulling the trigger at the right time, given the circumstances.

As you can see for yourself, the portfolio will become way more easy to analyze and value going forward. 95% of 2026 Net operating income (NOI) will be coming from a mix of A-tier office and Retail assets at premium locations. Mind you: the multifamily assets won't be leaving the premises, so the desirability of the locations won't vanish post-sale.

Let us dive into the dividend safety now. The dividend safety is certainly not going to be in the clear just yet. The AFFO pay-out ratio that includes recurring capital expenditures like property-related capex and non-cash net operating income adjustments (like straight-line rent), show you that we are at a payout ratio of 95%. You would like a REIT to be in the 60% - 85% range, depending on its lease structure and/or type of industry where it operates in. You demand a lower payout ratio for (mid-tier) office REITs and you can be fine with triple net lease structures. But that's another topic. Even though Armada Hoffler has A-tier assets (illustrated by the very high occupancy numbers reported this quarter again), a 50/50 blend of retail and office properties still warrants a more conservative payout ratio compared to net-REITs like W. P. Carey (WPC) or Realty Income (O). The payout ratio of 95% warrants a lower P/[A]FFO multiple in the coming years. But that's fine with me, since I don't invest in the short-term and neither should you.

The post-transformation AFFO includes a $0.05 per share non-cash stock-based compensation that is added back into the AFFO number. Excluding stock-based compensation, the dividend coverage - again - is extremely tight going forward. A potential (larger) share buyback can mitigate this impact, but we really have to see how this plays out.

Management provided a slide that shows the future [cash] NOI growth going forward. Management highlighted the potential for a $10 million annualized commercial NOI growth in the years ahead. An analyst on the CC asked the following:

... which implies around $150 million of acquisitions if you just assume 6.5% cap rate. So how do you plan to finance this?...

Management remained vague, implying that there will be debt capital available in addition to equity... but that would depend on the cost of equity...

The caveat is exactly that: the cost of equity. Time and time again, management said that they are not planning to reduce the dividend (again). Thankfully, they won't raise it either. The focus is on simplifying and deleveraging the company. But in the mean time, the dividend yield sits at 9%. Making it very expensive to make lucrative new investments. So the bullish NOI growth shown above only works when the share price climbs materially. I don't see this happening overnight. I believe that Mr. Market will have a "let's wait and see" approach regarding Armada for the foreseeable future.

Valuation

There are multiple ways to look at a REIT's valuation. I will use two multiples and a cap rate valuation to see where the intrinsic value per share lands for Armada.

P/[A]FFO multiple analysis

Back in August last year, I calculated the intrinsic value of AHH's common shares to be around $10.50 - $15.40, depending on your personal view of the company. Regarding the $15.40 price target, I wrote the following:

If you expect that Armada Hoffler will reach an investment-grade rating in a couple of years, all the combined efforts I summed up just now could theoretically lead to - let's say - a 14x P/FFO multiple. Since we are kind of guessing right now, this equals a 13% increase over the historic market valuation of this company. It seems reasonable to me in a bullish scenario.

I take the current 2025 FFO guidance at the high-point. Obviously the FFO will climb in the coming years, so we are still using a pretty depressed FFO to begin with. Even in this bullish scenario, I am trying not to be too extreme in my assumptions. But no matter how I look at it, you arrive at a fair value around $15.40 (14 x $1.10). A price target that I feel is very reasonable if you can hold on to your shares for at least 2 - 3 years.

I do think that my previous reasoning makes a lot of sense. But Armada Hoffler uses the NAREIT version of FFO in the 2026 guidance, that differentiates a lot from the normalized FFO that the company issues as well. During Q4 2025, AHH reported a 2025 Nareit FFO ($0.78) and its own 2025 Normalized FFO ($1.08). Ceteris Paribus, the 2026 Nareit FFO ($0.64) should equal a 2026 Normalized FFO of $0.89. Given the current (lowered) multiple of 12x that Fastgraph gives us (using the normalized FFO number), we arrive at a fair value of around $10.70. In line with my previous (conservative) price target of $10.50. Because we have to wait and see how the transition unfolds, going with the more conservative valuation approach is a prudent move.

But there is another way of looking at it. You can take the AFFO multiple that currently sits at 14.08x. When we take the 2026 AFFO guidance of $0.59, we arrive at a fair value of $8.30 per share.

Whether you take AFFO or FFO matters a lot regarding Armada Hoffler's valuation.

But it is important to understand the fact that we are using historic multiples and future [A]FFO numbers. The fact that the company's business model will be simplified, showing more predictable cashflow and a lower leverage, should warrant a higher multiple going forward. We are still talking about A-tier assets, illustrated by very high occupancy rates: Retail (94.9%) and office (96.4%). Even higher than the multifamily department (94.6%).

It is impossible right now to know where (and if) AHH's multiple will expand, but it makes sense to me if it does in the future.

Cap rate analysis

Regarding the 2026 guidance, management showed us the following (page 3):

  • $270 million in secured debt paydowns
  • $400 million in net unsecured debt paydowns
  • $50 million of Retail Properties with a cap rate range of 6.25% - 7%

When we take the midpoint of the 2026 NOI guidance, we arrive at $133.5 million.

We can assume that Armada Hoffler will make lucrative investments at attractive cap rates. Using the Q4 2025 numbers, the office segment showed a 150 bps higher occupancy rate compared to retail. The expected 2026 cash NOI growth rate also shows a faster growth. It makes sense to me to assume that the current cap rate of the blended retail/office portfolio should be around a 6% cap rate. Especially given the fact that new office supply is non-existent and AHH's assets are performing extremely well. When we use a 6% average cap rate on the entire post-transformation portfolio, the company value equals $2.23 billion.

The 2025 net debt including preferred equity came in at around $1.645 billion. When we subtract the debt paydowns of $670 million, we arrive at $975 million in net debt (including preferred equity). When we subtract the 2026 net debt from the total company value ($2.23 billion), we arrive at an equity value of $1.255 billion. The current market cap sits around $640 million (at $6.16 a share - at close on Feb. 18). To be conservative, I will take a 15% discount to the equity value/net asset value per share, we arrive at a value of $10.20 per share. The conservative value per share should be around $8.30 - $10.70. Shares could expand by around 35% - 74%. In the meantime, you get paid a 9% dividend.

Keep in mind that it makes sense for Armada to trade at a higher future P/[A]FFO multiple because of the lower leverage, predictable cashflow and high quality portfolio. During the conference call, it became clear as well that many leases have below-market rent structures. Meaning that future rent growth can become pretty meaningful. It also means that there is a very low probability that future rents will decrease, like we've seen with REITs like Medical Properties Trust (MPT) or Brandywine Realty Trust (BDN).

Conclusion

It is hard to know how the market will ultimately respond to Armada's company transition. I am a firm believer that shares will trade materially higher in the coming years. But I do have to say that the dividend coverage is tight. Some investors might not like the fact that the multifamily part of the portfolio is getting sold and future cash NOI growth depends on the cost of equity, which is high at the moment and will hammer per share FFO growth going forward.

AHH will be a smaller, more conservatively led, and leveraged REIT going forward. It is hard to tell how this will resonate with the investor base in the near future.

For those investors that have a long-term investment horizon, just look at the graph below and tell me what will happen in the years ahead. Will re-leasing spreads on AHH's A-tier retail and office properties be high or low? And will cap rates expand or fall because of the upcoming supply/demand mismatch? I think that most of us know the answer. All it takes right now is patience.

Successful investing takes time, discipline and patience. No matter how great the talent or effort, some things just take time: You can't produce a baby in one month by getting nine women pregnant.

As always, thanks for reading.

data.title: Angie Holdings Limited Targets IPO On Faltering Revenue Growth, Thin Capitalization
data.description:
Angie Holdings’ Financial Results Are Worsening

Angie Holdings Limited (AGIE) has filed proposed terms for a $17.7 million US IPO, according to an amended F-1/A registration statement.

The company sells corporate uniforms to customers located in Hong Kong, China.

AGIE’s revenue growth has stalled, it has thin capitalization, its net results are deteriorating, and management is seeking excessive valuation multiples at IPO.

My outlook on the AGIE IPO is to sell (avoid it).

What Does Angie Holdings Do?

The firm designs and sells a wide variety of uniforms for schools, businesses, and other commercial applications in Hong Kong.

Angie was founded in 2015 and is led by Chairman and CEO Mr. Wai Sing FONG, who has developed a collaboration with PolyU to create improved heat-resistant clothing, which is especially relevant for the construction and related industries.

The firm’s offerings include:

  • Corporate uniforms

  • Design services

  • Tailoring services

  • Functionality counseling

The company's revenue contribution by product type and location is shown in the pie charts below:

However, the company's customer base is significantly concentrated, with only five customers accounting for 31% of total revenue for the first half of 2025.

Also, Angie does not typically have long-term contracts with its customer base, so customers have lower switching costs, presenting a higher risk of loss to the firm as a result.

What Is Angie’s Market?

The global workwear market was an estimated $19.2 billion in 2025 and is forecasted to exceed $28 billion by 2033.

If accomplished, the CAGR of the worldwide market would be 4.9% from 2026 to 2033.

The Hong Kong market would likely be well under 1% of the total global market, although the larger Asia Pacific region accounted for the largest share of the global market, at around 44.7% in 2025.

By application type, the construction workwear market, which is the focus for Angie, accounted for 23.75% in 2025.

The growth of the market is expected due to increasing industrialization, continued urban infrastructure development, and growing demand for branded and customizable workwear solutions.

The historical and projected future growth path of the worldwide workwear market is shown in the chart below by type of product:

The company faces over 200 competitors in the Hong Kong market alone, including integrated providers and industry-specific vendors.

Competitor companies include:

  • The Group

  • iGift

  • K&S Uniform Company

  • Antonhill Company

  • Easy-Order

  • 101 Uniform Company

  • Workwear

  • MCSportsWear & Uniform

  • Beverly Apparel

  • Others

The company's strengths include its asset-light business model, strategic R&D partnership with Hong Kong Polytechnic, and customization expertise.

Its weaknesses are customer and geographic concentration, dependency on third-party manufacturers, and small size.

Management believes its opportunities lie in the potential for overseas expansion, a growing overall demand for functional apparel, and integrating technologies into its supply chain and products.

Threats include intense competition from hundreds of competitors in the Hong Kong market, regulatory and political risks regarding PRC implementation of laws in Hong Kong, and supply chain disruptions.

Angie’s Recent Financial Results

The company's topline revenue growth results have been disappointing, with either an outright contraction or slight growth in recent periods.

Operating and net profit have also dropped, and cash flow from operations has been uneven, as the table shows here:

Angie’s IPO Plan

The company intends to sell 2.73 million shares of common stock at a proposed midpoint price of $6.50 per share, for total gross proceeds of approximately $17.7 million, excluding underwriter allotments.

No existing shareholders or potentially new large shareholders have indicated an interest in the IPO, at least as disclosed in the IPO filing.

Company, director, and shareholder (greater than 5%) lockups will be for 180 days after the closing of the offering.

Management intends to use the net proceeds of the IPO as follows:

Approximately 25% for R&D of new functional materials;

Approximately 25% for R&D in using AI and/or software or mobile application to enhance automation of operation;

Approximately 20% for branding and marketing;

Approximately 15% for setting up representative offices in foreign countries such as France, Singapore and Dubai; and

The remaining 15% to fund working capital and for other general corporate purposes.

(Source: SEC)

The sole listed underwriter of the IPO is Cathay Securities, a firm that frequently seeks to bring small Asian companies public in the US.

Capital And Liquidity Dynamics

As of June 30, 2025, AGIE had $27,482 in cash and $1.9 million in total liabilities.

For the trailing twelve-month period ending June 30, 2025, AGIE generated free cash flow of just over $1 million on zero capex spend.

But with management’s focus on R&D spending with the proceeds, that will likely change or result in greater opex spending.

Prior to the IPO, the company's liabilities have been growing, while cash trends have been downward, suggesting a motivation for the IPO being to recapitalize the firm.

So, although the company generated over $1 million in free cash flow in the trailing twelve months, it has likely devoted the bulk of that to inventory growth and credit extension to customers, among other uses.

Angie’s Growth Is Stalled And Profits Are Dropping

Angie’s financial results are deteriorating just as it is seeking US public capital market investment, a negative signal for prospective investors.

The company's topline revenue growth has evaporated or turned negative in recent years, while operating and net results have worsened in the most recent half-year period.

Management seeks to grow through developing new functional materials via its increased R&D spend, expanding its overseas presence, and offering related services.

AGIE is exposed to all manner of risks, from customer concentration, geographic concentration, unpredictable Chinese regulatory actions, intense competition, and expansion risks.

It operates in a market that is expected to grow only moderately, and management plans to use most of the IPO’s proceeds for R&D expenses, which may not bear fruit for some time.

The company is also a ‘foreign private issuer’ and an ‘emerging growth company.’ These designations allow management to produce less information for shareholders.

Many of these types of company stocks have performed poorly immediately after their IPO, and Asian firms in particular have shown a habit of communicating very little with investors.

Also, a number of these firms have subsequently delisted their stock not long after their IPO, further destroying shareholder value.

Management is asking IPO investors to pay an EV/EBITDA multiple of nearly 48x on falling operating and net profits, surely an excessive ask.

With faltering revenue growth, worsening operating and net results, thin capitalization, and excessive valuation expectations, my outlook on the AGIE IPO is to sell (avoid it).

Expected IPO Pricing Date: To be announced

data.title: Workday: Oversold With Material Upside Potential
data.description:
Business software company Workday, Inc. (WDAY) is currently trading at one of its lowest positions in years. The declines follow a rough stretch for the broader tech sector, and specifically the software names, in the wake of growing fears and realizations regarding the impact of AI on individual business models.

Shares in WDAY are down over 30% YTD. This includes an over 20% decline in the past month alone.

I believe the pullback presents an attractive opportunity for investors seeking discounted positioning in companies with durable business models that likely are going through a period of transitory fear rather than a more existential crisis, in my view.

At current trading valuations, I can see shares in WDAY ultimately regaining their price point near the upper end of the stock's 52-week range. This would imply almost 100% upside potential. While this may sound almost fantastical, I surely believe an opportunity for outperformance is to be had.

WDAY Stock Key Metrics

Workday’s stock currently commands a forward trading multiple of just 15.5x earnings. The sector median is about 23x, in line with the broader S&P 500 (SPY), while WDAY’s five-year average is in the mid-40x range. For a company in the software space, the current valuation is practically pennies on the dollar.

WDAY is set to release its Q4 results next week, and if history is any guide, WDAY’s chances of beating expectations are quite good. The company has never missed consensus estimates since Q1 of FY23. This is on both the top and bottom lines. Given the recent selloff to multi-year lows, shares could see a considerable bounce if results and guidance landed even slightly better than expected.

Though shares are lightly covered by the Seeking Alpha (“SA”) community, those that have provided coverage have leaned bullish. Coverage is more widespread among Wall Street analysts, and the data here is somewhat shocking. For example, of the 43 analysts rating the stock over the last 90 days, 55% of the population have rated shares as a ‘strong buy.’

Furthermore, this has been paired with an average price target of about $250/share. That would imply 75% upside potential from current trading levels. While WDAY does have a mountain of negative sentiment to climb, I believe this is attainable.

WDAY Operating Performance

If you strip away the noise around AI fears, Workday’s actual operating performance tells a very different story.

Granted, Q4 results are pending release, but based on Q3, operations appear solid. In Q3, the company delivered 15% subscription revenue growth. This contributed to an increase in total revenues of 13% during the period. Non-GAAP operating margins, meanwhile, came in at an impressive 28.5%. For a company supposedly facing “existential” pressure, those are hardly distress signals, in my view.

Even more encouraging is the backlog strength. Current remaining performance obligations (“cRPO”) grew 17.6%, and total subscription backlog now sits at nearly +$26B, up 17%. Furthermore, gross revenue retention held steady at 97%. This is a case in point of the stickiness of the platform.

It’s important to note that AI is complementary to WDAY and not a replacement for it. More than 75% of core customers, for example, are using Workday Illuminate AI, driving over 1 billion AI actions this year. In addition, AI products contributed over 1.5 points of ARR growth in the quarter alone.

Cash flow was also strong, with operating cash flow up 45% in Q3, and full-year free cash flow expected to grow 23%.

Simply put, the fundamentals remain durable, profitable, and growing, not deteriorating.

What Is The Outlook For WDAY Stock?

With Q4 results on deck, here’s what to expect. For Q4, the management team had previously laid out expectations for 15% YOY subscription revenue growth. And for the full fiscal year, subscription revenue is projected to reach +$8.8B, up 14%. In my view, that’s steady, durable expansion in a macro environment that many would argue remains uneven.

Importantly, backlog trends continue to support forward visibility. cRPO is expected to grow 15%–16% in Q4, reflecting solid demand momentum even as the company laps tougher comparisons.

Management is also targeting a 12%–15% subscription revenue CAGR through FY28, with FY27 growth expected around 13%. I view those as healthy growth rates for a company now operating at WDAY's scale.

Margins are expected to continue moving in the right direction as well. Full-year non-GAAP operating margin is expected to approach 29%, alongside free cash flow growth of roughly 23%.

When I step back, the outlook doesn’t reflect a business under structural threat. It reflects a scaled software leader still compounding revenue in double digits while expanding margins. This, in my view, is a combination that typically commands a premium, not a discount multiple.

Is WDAY Stock A Buy, Sell, Or Hold?

I view shares in WDAY as a ‘strong buy’ at current levels. The stock is trading as if growth is about to materially decelerate or AI is about to disrupt its core business model. Yet the numbers simply don’t support that narrative, in my view.

We’re looking at double-digit subscription growth, expanding margins, strong backlog visibility, and accelerating AI monetization, all at roughly 15x forward earnings. That disconnect is hard to ignore.

With Q4 earnings due next week, I believe the company will meet, and potentially exceed, expectations. WDAY has had a consistent track record of beats on revenues and earnings, and even a modest beat on revenue or guidance could trigger a meaningful bounce given how compressed sentiment currently is across the software sector. I believe that when positioning is this negative, it doesn’t take perfection to spark a rebound.

That said, there is risk. If WDAY were to miss expectations, the market could punish shares further given the fragile tone surrounding AI-exposed software names.

Still, when I weigh the downside risk against the potential upside, including a move back toward consensus price targets in the $250/share range over the medium to long term, I see the risk/reward skewed favorably to the upside. To me, shares look oversold, not broken.

data.title: Pure Storage: Quality Business, Expensive Certainty
data.description:
I’m initiating coverage on Pure Storage, Inc. (PSTG) at a time when it looks like the company is sitting at an interesting intersection. On the one hand, it is a structural winner in enterprise storage; think flash-only architecture, a recurring revenue model, exposure to AI workloads, and improving operating margins. On the other hand, it is still operating in a cyclical enterprise IT spending environment where long periods of digestion can stall a business’s momentum very quickly.

As such, the question now is whether it is a secular compounder in a cyclical industry or a cyclical hardware vendor that looks better during strong demand phases. As with most things in life, I believe it’s a mix of both, and I’m going with a Hold at this time.

First, though, I want to talk about the company itself.

Structural Moat: I See Real Edges, But Not Immunity

Pure Storage, Inc. was founded in 2009 and went public in 2015. Today, it serves more than 14,000 customers globally, including over 60% of the Fortune 500, and it employs over 6,000 people. It is a “leading provider of enterprise-grade, all-flash block, file, and object storage,” so we’re not talking about a niche flash vendor fighting for relevance. This is a multi-billion-dollar enterprise infrastructure vendor that’s competing directly with big names like Dell, NetApp, and Hewlett-Packard Enterprise (HPE) and winning more market share in all-flash arrays.

Flash-Only Architecture

I believe that its flash-only strategy is still a core differentiator. PSTG’s entire product stack is optimized around flash, and that is because it hasn’t had to defend a legacy disk portfolio. We can see that clarity in its proprietary DirectFlash modules and tight hardware-software integration. No other company controls performance, endurance, and data efficiency the way PSTG does across the stack.

I think this matters most in AI-driven workloads for a few reasons. For one, AI infrastructure isn’t forgiving when it comes to latency or throughput. Flash density and power efficiency are constraints in that environment, and the company’s product architecture is built to cater to that demand trend, especially now.

Evergreen//One And Customer Retention

Besides the technological moat, there’s Evergreen//One, its storage-as-a-service (STaaS) platform. Here’s how the company describes it:

“Evergreen//One™ is a storage-as-a-service (STaaS) platform that unifies on-premises and public-cloud storage resources into a single data storage subscription delivering a true hybrid-cloud experience.”

It allows customers to basically upgrade their controllers within the same chassis every few years, and that means they don’t have to deal with switching friction.

The convenience also means that the customers typically stick with the company for longer, and their lifetime value is much higher. So, the business now has a growing services and subscription revenue stream, and that’s part of why its annual recurring revenue (ARR) in Q3 FY26 was around $1.8 billion, a 17% increase YoY.

Competitive Positioning

PSTG competes in a highly consolidated enterprise storage market. I mentioned some of its competitors earlier, and some of them, like Dell and HPE, benefit from the added advantage of having broader server and infrastructure bundles. Then there’s NetApp, which has more entrenched enterprise relationships and hybrid cloud tooling.

PSTG’s biggest advantage in this industry is its focus. It builds single-architecture flash, a clean operating system, and has stronger customer satisfaction scores, which I can tell from its ARR and net revenue retention numbers.

The company is one of the biggest players in the all-flash array market today, and it holds around 12% of global market share, according to Porter’s Five Forces. From here, the question is about whether it can continue grabbing more market share as the market is maturing, and it has to factor in hyperscaler purchasing.

Its Growth Profile Is Strong

If you look at PSTG’s growth over the last half-decade or so, since COVID hit, you’ll see a company that has been growing very steadily. Its revenue has gone from the mid-$1B range in 2019 and 2020 to nearly $3.2 billion in FY2025, and management expects another jump to >$3.6 billion in FY2026. The business is also doing a decent job of staying profitable, albeit the stock has been very volatile over the last year.

Now, the growth looks linear on paper, but the company has had hiccups along the way. Things like enterprise pullbacks, hyperscaler pauses, and other issues have been present. Remember that this is still largely an infrastructure-spend business model that is just upping its recurring revenue via a subscription model.

That is why I think the growth is even more impressive. The sales mix is doing a lot of the heavy lifting on this front, and I believe that it will continue to change over the next few years. Subscription services are a much more profitable segment for any business because the overhead required is much less than what you need when running a hardware-focused business. So, the higher margins are coming from this segment, even when PSTG is dealing with slower product demand.

For context, the company’s gross margin has consistently stayed in the high-60s to low-70s range over the aforementioned period, but the revenue from subscriptions is what has allowed PSTG to become profitable on an operating and net income basis over the last few years. I should point out that some of those improvements have come from the hardware side too, and it’s clear that the company is using its pricing power and data efficiency very well.

Finally, we have hyperscaler exposure, which is where I think PSTG has to contend with some cyclicality. The company's topline grows quickly when there’s more demand from the hyperscalers, and the opposite is true when that demand slows down to allow them to digest the capacity. That’s why I still see the overall growth as secular, but with some cyclicality baked into the model.

Now, I’ll discuss the numbers from PSTG’s Q3 earnings report.

Financial Quality And Capital Allocation

72%+ gross margin in a hardware-involved infrastructure business is not exactly normal, but I have discussed how the software layer, data efficiency, and installed base expansion are doing the real work here.

I also touched on the ARR earlier, and at $1.8B and RPO at $2.9B, we can see how much the company’s recurring base helps with forward visibility. We can reasonably expect significant portions of those figures to reduce the binary nature of hardware orders every quarter.

The company is already profitable on a GAAP basis, but I think the non-GAAP numbers present an even more impressive picture of how it is executing today. A 20% operating margin is a clear sign that PTSG is now enjoying some benefits of scale, and that’s a very different profile from the low-double-digit margin infrastructure vendors that rely purely on volume.

In addition to that, I like that the business is structurally cash-generative and it has a clean balance sheet. PSTG ended Q3 with a bit over $1.5B in cash and marketable securities, less than $100M in debt, and it hasn’t drawn on any revolver. Being in such a liquid position gives management a lot of flexibility, and I suppose that explains why they’re actively returning capital to shareholders. On that front, they repurchased about $53M of stock in Q3 and authorized an additional $400M buyback in December. That tells me that they view the equity as investable, and that’s good news if you’re a shareholder.

You're Paying A Big Premium For Execution Here

Today, PSTG trades at around:

  • 6.3x forward sales.

  • 37x forward earnings.

  • 29x forward EV/EBITDA.

That is premium territory by every possible metric, and I think the stock is overpriced because the market now expects durability, stable margins, and ARR growth.

I’m not arguing that the business doesn’t deserve a premium, because it does, especially because its profile is much better than that of its legacy storage peers. But at 37x earnings, there’s no room for disappointment.

Let’s look at its EPS, for example. For FY26, the expectation is that the EPS will land around $1.95. FY27’s estimate is closer to $2.3. Today’s price already assumes that the business’s current trajectory will not change. But if we see slower growth, around mid-single digits, and the market decides that the stock deserves something closer to 24-26x earnings, you’re suddenly looking at a share price closer to $55-60 without any decline in earnings. That is going off the multiple compressions alone.

Also, 29x EV/EBITDA is a multiple that I’d reserve for companies that have clean, visible, and durable growth. I’m sure that PSTG will become that over the next few years, but I don’t think it has all three just yet. These multiples just scream execution risk to me, and I cannot make a case for buying into the company at this price.

Q4 And FY26 Preview: The Multiple Is On The Line

Q4 revenue should land around the $1.02–1.04B range based on prior guidance, and that’s fine. I’m more interested in the sales mix than the headline number. I want to see whether product revenue stabilized or if hyperscaler demand is still lumpy, and ARR is the bigger tell for me.

It was $1.8B last quarter, up 17%, and if ARR growth drops into low-teens territory, it would be very difficult for me to point to durability as a lever for the stock’s re-rating. I believe that at this valuation, recurring growth needs to stay well in the mid-teens, and maybe even higher.

Besides that, RPO also needs to grow faster than revenue because it underlines how much of the company’s business we can model predictably. Then, there are the margins. PSTG is doing well on the profitability front, and its non-GAAP operating margin of 20% is a solid anchor. So, if the company reports margin compression in Q4 or the word from management regarding FY27 guidance implies that we might see some margin drift, I don’t think the stock will be forgiven. At nearly 30x EV/EBITDA, I don’t see how it will get the benefit of the doubt.

Ideally, we should be looking for signs that the company can grow its revenue by double digits again while expanding its recurring base and keeping margins steady. I don’t think investors are looking for another breakout year or quarter just yet. The stock is already expensive, but stable growth may keep it ticking along nicely for a while.

I like the company, but I’m neutral on its stock at this price. I think Q4 will really test the premium, or it just might help to justify it a bit more.

data.title: Bausch + Lomb Q4 Earnings Review: Still A Buy, With Some Caveats
data.description:
Investment Overview

Bausch + Lomb Corporation (BLCO), a leading global eye health specialist and marketer and seller of ~400 products in total (source: Q3 2025 10-Q filing), announced its Q4 and full-year earnings on February 18th.

The company earned $1.4bn in revenues in Q4, up 7% on a constant currency basis, and reported a net loss of $(58m) and adjusted EBITDA of $326m. Across the whole of 2025, revenues earned amounted to $5.1bn, GAAP net loss attributed to the business was $(360m), and adjusted EBITDA came to $858m, or $891m excluding IPR&D. Revenue grew by 5% on a constant currency basis.

At the end of yesterday, shares were down <0.5%, valued at $17.7 per share, and BLCO's market cap valuation currently stands at $6.2bn.

BLCO operates three core divisions—Vision Care, which covers its consumer and contact lens products; Surgical, which includes implantables, equipment, and consumables; and Pharmaceuticals. In 2025, these three divisions earned, respectively, $2.92bn, up 6% year-on-year; $894m, up 4%; and $1.28bn, up 6%.

In terms of guidance for 2026, BLCO shares the following table in its earnings presentation:

Analysis: No Fresh Sale News, But Plenty Of Fresh Impetus

In a note shared on Seeking Alpha last year, I covered BLCO's Q4 and full-year 2024 results and provided some background on the company, its prior acquisition by Valeant Pharma—now known as Bausch Health Companies (BHC)—and its subsequent spin-out from BHC, as that company ran into difficulties.

I won't go over that same ground here; suffice it to share a statement from BLCO's Q3 2025 10-Q filing as follows:

Bausch + Lomb is a subsidiary of Bausch Health Companies Inc. (“BHC”), with BHC directly or indirectly holding 310,449,643 Bausch + Lomb common shares, which represents approximately 88% of the issued and outstanding common shares of Bausch + Lomb, as of October 22, 2025.

On August 6, 2020, BHC announced its plan to separate our eye health business into an independent publicly traded entity, separate from the remainder of BHC (the “Separation”). This resulted in the initial public offering of Bausch + Lomb (the “B+L IPO”), and our common shares began trading on the New York Stock Exchange and the Toronto Stock Exchange, in each case under the ticker symbol “BLCO”, on May 6, 2022.

Bausch + Lomb understands that BHC continues to believe that completing the Separation, which may include the transfer of all or a portion of BHC’s remaining direct or indirect equity interest in Bausch + Lomb to its shareholders (the “Distribution”), the monetization of all or a portion of BHC’s ownership interest in Bausch + Lomb, the sale of the Company (a “Sale Transaction”) or a combination thereof, makes strategic sense and that BHC continues to evaluate all relevant factors and considerations related to completing the Separation, including those factors described in BHC’s public filings.

Last February, BLCO released a statement as follows:

Taking Bausch + Lomb private with a third-party buyer was one of several options being explored to complete a full separation from Bausch Health Companies Inc. After engagement with potential buyers, that process is complete, and will not result in a transaction at this time.

While BHC may wish to sell its BLCO holding—or part of its holding—to pay down some of its debt, it ultimately did not do so last year, although Bausch Health's management did address the situation on its Q4 earnings call yesterday as follows:

So when it comes to the monetization of our B&L equity stake, really all options are on the table. I think what will guide really our monetization decisions, as we said in our prepared remarks, is really shareholder value creation.

BLCO did share a detailed overview of its ambitions for the period 2025-2028 in a presentation given at its investor day last November, however, as shown below:

BLCO's CEO Brent Saunders struck a bullish tone on the company's prospects in the near and long term on the Q4 earnings call, telling analysts:

When you hear me talk about execution today, this is exactly what I mean. In the fourth quarter, we didn't just grow. We grew smarter and faster than the market. When you see 7% constant currency revenue growth and 27% adjusted EBITDA growth, that's real operating leverage and a clear sign of our commitment to financial excellence.

As we can see in the chart above, the company appears to have driven growth almost across the board. Vision Care revenues only grew 5% year-on-year in Q4, but in Q4 2024, the division had grown in double-digit percentages. Growth drivers include contact lenses, surely a robust long-term business, and OTC dry eye products.

Long-term, not only does management expect to drive 5-7% growth to 2028, but it also believes it has an exciting contact lens pipeline that delivers ~$1.2bn in revenues, as illustrated in the slide below:

In Pharmaceuticals, the dry eye portfolio also delivered, with Miebo and Xiidra driving >$600m in revenues between them, although we may note that BLCO rival Alcon (ALC) secured approval for a new therapy, TRIPTYR, last year. With that said, this is a multi-billion dollar market, and during a presentation at the J.P. Morgan Healthcare conference in January, management noted:

We're developing the first dual action eye drop to treat dry eye combining the distinct mechanisms of the active ingredients of Miebo and Xiidra to address tier evaporation and inflammation in a single therapy. Our glaucoma pipeline asset would be the first to lower intraocular pressure and improved visual acuity, resetting the treatment for this market.

In Surgical, despite a wobble in April last year when BLCO announced a voluntary recall of several brands of intraocular lenses marketed under its enVista brand in response to reports of adverse events, described as "an increased number of reports of toxic anterior segment syndrome (TASS)" in a company announcement, the outlook remains promising, in my view, with management noting:

We anticipate the premium intraocular lenses will continue to be the primary growth driver for our Surgical business, and we believe we are well positioned to capture market share following significant portfolio expansion over the last few years

Similarly to the other two divisions, the surgical pipeline is promising—an overview of upcoming pipeline highlights was provided in the presentation as follows:

Analysis: Is BLCO Presenting Buy Signals?

Last February, I gave BLCO stock a Buy rating, noting that a >$5bn revenue per annum business that is close to breakeven deserves a far higher valuation than $5.6bn (its value at the time), and I would repeat that same thesis for 2026 and beyond also. A $5.5bn revenue business rarely trades at <$10bn market cap unless there are significant threats to the business, in my experience.

As per its 2025 10-K filing, BLCO reported cash and equivalents of $383m, total current assets of $2.98bn, current portion of long-term debt of $39m, and long-term debt and other financial liabilities of $5bn.

BLCO says (in its investor day presentation from November last year) that it is targeting a net leverage (to adjusted EBITDA) of ~3.5x by the end of 2028 and an investment-grade debt rating, defining net leverage as:

the ratio of net debt (which is calculated as total debt (its GAAP equivalent) less cash and cash equivalents) over Adjusted EBITDA (excluding Acquired IPR&D)

Personally, I feel more comfortable about a company's prospects when the GAAP and adjusted are not too far apart, and many elements of BLCO's revenue and profit forecasts are on an adjusted basis; therefore, I detect a slight red flag waving. For example, a breakdown of non-GAAP EPS adjustments taken from the earnings presentation is shown below:

With that said, with the revenues it generates, and its adjusted EBITDA margin of 23.5%, so long as BLCO keeps chipping away at its debt, and delivers the mid-to-high single digit growth it is promising, its share price and valuation ought to increase in value, to a point where the price to sales ratio climbs towards ~2x, at least, and with the projected double-digit adjusted EPS growth, perhaps profitability on a GAAP basis can be achieved also, and we all have a positive price to earnings ratio also.

As such, and despite some caveats discussed above, I am upholding my Buy rating on BLCO stock. After my last Buy call, when shares were worth ~$16, the stock sank as low as $11 (buffeted by the Trump administration's Liberation Day tariffs, I'd suspect, given this is a global business), but has subsequently recovered, and I believe we are set for more growth in 2026.

data.title: AAII Sentiment Survey: Neutral Sentiment Leaps
data.description:
Neutral sentiment among individual investors about the short-term outlook for stocks increased in the latest AAII Sentiment Survey. Meanwhile, optimism and pessimism decreased.

Bullish sentiment, expectations that stock prices will rise over the next six months, decreased 4.0 percentage points to 34.5%. Bullish sentiment is below its historical average of 37.5% for the first time in 12 weeks.

Neutral sentiment, expectations that stock prices will stay essentially unchanged over the next six months, increased 5.2 percentage points to 28.5%. Neutral sentiment is below its historical average of 31.5% for the 83rd time in 85 weeks.

Bearish sentiment, expectations that stock prices will fall over the next six months, decreased 1.2 percentage points to 36.9%. Bearish sentiment is above its historical average of 31.0% for the fifth time in 12 weeks.

The bull-bear spread (bullish minus bearish sentiment) decreased 2.8 percentage points to –2.4%. The bull-bear spread is below its historical average of 6.5% for the second time in 12 weeks.

This week’s special question asked AAII members how they prefer companies to return cash to shareholders.

Here is how they responded:

  • I like companies that use both dividends and buybacks: 40.8%
  • I prefer dividends for the regular income stream: 37.2%
  • I prefer stock buybacks for tax efficiency: 12.4%
  • Not sure/no opinion: 8.7%

This week’s Sentiment Survey results:

  • Bullish: 34.5%, down 4.0 percentage points
  • Neutral: 28.5%, up 5.2 percentage points
  • Bearish: 36.9%, down 1.2 percentage points

Historical averages:

  • Bullish: 37.5%
  • Neutral: 31.5%
  • Bearish: 31.0%

The AAII Sentiment Survey has been conducted weekly since July 1987. The survey and its results are available online.

data.title: First Eagle Small Cap Opportunity Fund Q4 2025 Portfolio Review
data.description:
The following segment was excerpted from the First Eagle Small Cap Opportunity Fund Q4 2025 Commentary.

Small Cap Opportunity Fund A Shares (without sales charge*) posted a return of 1.71% in fourth quarter 2025. Healthcare, materials and information technology were the leading contributors among equity sectors; consumer discretionary and communication services detracted and energy was flat. The Fund underperformed the Russell 2000 Value Index in the period.

Leading contributors in the First Eagle Small Cap Opportunity Fund this quarter included Hecla Mining Company (HL), Axogen, Inc. (AXGN), FormFactor, Inc. (FORM), Kaiser Aluminum Corporation (KALU) and OUTFRONT Media Inc (OUT).

Hecla Mining is the largest producer of silver in the US and Canada. The company reported better-than-expected earnings for its most recent quarter, driven by above-consensus production volume and improved efficiency. Higher commodity prices have also been a tailwind. We think the company is a high-quality operator, and we like Hecla's focus on strengthening its balance sheet.

Axogen develops products and technologies for nerve regeneration and repair. The company reported better-than-expected results for its most recent quarter. During the quarter, the Food and Drug Administration also approved a biologics license application for the company's primary product, Avance Nerve Graft. The license provides 12 years of market exclusivity in the US and paves the way for broader insurance coverage, which may increase the commercial potential of the product.

FormFactor designs and manufactures testing and measurement equipment, with an emphasis on probe cards that allow chipmakers to test products at the wafer level before they are packaged and installed in devices like cell phones. The company reported better-than-expected results for its most recent quarter as demand for FormFactor's testing products grew due to the increasing level and complexity of testing as the density and complexity of chips escalates.

Kaiser Aluminum manufactures fabricated aluminum products for aerospace and defense, beverage and food packaging, and automotive and general engineering. The company reported better-than-expected results for its most recent quarter, driven by improved pricing and sales mix in premium markets, like aerospace and packaging. We believe the company can continue to grow sales and margins as facility and product lines expansions become fully operational, serving the aerospace and coated packaging markets, respectively.

Outfront Media is an advertising company that has billboards and transit displays. The company has been converting traditional static billboard advertising to digital displays, which has brought in new advertisers and driven growth. One of the company's key contracts is with the New York Metropolitan Transit Authority (MTA). The digital displays have been generating more revenue per display because multiple advertisements are able to run in rotation with dynamic content updates, as well as increasing ridership in the MTA.

The leading detractors in the quarter were Treace Medical Concepts, Inc. (TMCI), Thryv Holdings, Inc. (THRY), Backblaze, Inc. Class A (BLZE), Janus International Group, Inc. (JBI) and Universal Technical Institute, Inc (UTI).

Treace Medical is an orthopedic medical device company focused on bunion and midfoot surgery. Shares have been pressured by share loss to less invasive procedures. With a large addressable market and Treace's own move toward less invasive systems, our investment thesis remains intact. The company is improving its operating efficiency and has a strong balance sheet.

Thryv provides a software platform for customer relationship management and digital and print marketing services for small businesses. Weakness during the quarter reflected challenges upselling legacy customers to its software services. Because Thryv remains committed to cross-selling to higher-margin services, it is applying ample cash flows from legacy activities to strengthen its balance sheet.

Backblaze provides cloud storage and computer backup solutions. After a strong first half in 2025, decelerating growth trends in cloud storage among their target customer base of AI-related startups emerged alongside changed corporate accounting practices. Given the lack of clarity for the company going forward, we exited our position.

Janus is a global manufacturer and supplier of solutions for the self-storage industry, providing doors, hallway systems, mobile storage units, automation systems and smart-entry technologies. data.title: First Eagle Small Cap Opportunity Fund Q4 2025 Commentary
data.description:
Market Overview

The good news is that 2025 was the third consecutive year of gains by small cap stocks. The bad news is that it was the fifth consecutive year that small caps lagged larger names.

The Russell 2000 Index's 2.2% advance in the fourth quarter contributed to an annual total return of 12.8%; this compares to respective gains of 2.7% and 17.9% by the S&P 500 Index. That said, we are encouraged by the momentum evident in the small cap market. Notably, the Russell 2000 has rallied more than 42% since hitting its 2025 low in early April, outpacing the S&P 500 by nearly 375 basis points. 1

Have the Winds Begun to Shift in Favor of Small Caps?

Smaller company stocks in general have appeared cheap for some time, in our estimation, and history has taught us that they can stay this way for an extended period before the market recognizes their value. As fundamental investors, we believe that earnings growth is among the more durable catalysts for such recognition and can help boost performance even without multiple expansion. There are signs that a resurgence in small cap earnings may at last be underway after a long period in the shadow of the market's larger names.

Amid a backdrop of 4.3% GDP growth and manageable inflation, Russell 2000 year-over-year growth in the third quarter came in at nearly 64%, trouncing the consensus estimate heading into reporting season. Forecasts call for a similar level of growth in the fourth quarter, bringing calendar 2025 earnings growth to about 45%—well above the S&P 500's 13% growth. This trend is expected to persist through periods for which estimates have been published, with the Russell 2000 forecast to deliver earnings growth of 63% in 2026 and 32% in 2027 compared to 16% and 15%, respectively, for the S&P 500. Similarly, the small cap revenue growth rate is forecast to improve over the next several quarters and ultimately surpass that of large cap stocks. 2

Helping support improved small company earnings has been the lower cost—and greater availability—of capital. Smaller companies tend to be far more reliant on short-term, floating-rate debt compared to large cap names. Since the Federal Reserve's rate-cut cycle began in

September 2024, the secured overnight financing rate (SOFR)—the reference rate for many smaller borrowers' loans—declined more than 160 basis points, providing companies carrying variable-rate debt with hard-dollar savings. Because SOFR trades very closely with the federal funds rate, additional rate cuts in 2026—futures markets suggest the fed funds rate will be 25 basis points lower at midyear and 50 basis points lower by year end—could provide small companies with further savings on interest expenses. 3

More stable interest rates could also help support the market for initial public offerings (IPOs), which has been slowly coming back to life after cratering in 2022 with the onset of Fed rate hikes. Even amid 2025's tariff volatility and government shutdown, 202 IPOs were priced during the year, a four-year high and 35% increase from 2024. 4 Given an administration that has demonstrated a hands-off approach to regulatory matters, continued improvement in IPO activity during 2026 would not come as a surprise, especially as financial buyers look to monetize the investments they have held for several years.

A Tentative First Step

The strong rally in smaller stocks following the post-Liberation Day swoon reminded us that this beast still has claws. 5 Despite this recent show of strength, longer-term performance trends remain skewed toward large names, with the relative performance of small caps remaining near previous cyclical troughs. 6

Respecting the tendency for reversion to the mean, our confidence in eventual strong sustained returns from small caps—driven by fundamentals—remains firm. In the meantime, we'll remain focused on ensuring our portfolio is cheaper than the market and well-positioned for upside should recent momentum persist.

  1. 1. Source: FactSet; data as of December 31, 2025.
  2. 2. Source: LSEG I/B/E/S; data as of January 9, 2026.
  3. 3. Source: CME FedWatch; data as of January 12, 2026.
  4. 4. Source: Renaissance Capital; data as of January 2, 2026
  5. 5. Source: FactSet; data as of December 31, 2025.
  6. 6. Source: Furey Research Partners, FactSet; data as of November 28, 2025.

Portfolio Review

Small Cap Opportunity Fund A Shares (without sales charge*) posted a return of 1.71% in fourth quarter 2025. Healthcare, materials and information technology were the leading contributors among equity sectors; consumer discretionary and communication services detracted and energy was flat. The Fund underperformed the Russell 2000 Value Index in the period.

Leading contributors in the First Eagle Small Cap Opportunity Fund this quarter included Hecla Mining Company (HL), Axogen, Inc. (AXGN), FormFactor, Inc. (FORM), Kaiser Aluminum Corporation (KALU) and OUTFRONT Media Inc (OUT).

Hecla Mining is the largest producer of silver in the US and Canada. The company reported better-than-expected earnings for its most recent quarter, driven by above-consensus production volume and improved efficiency. Higher commodity prices have also been a tailwind. We think the company is a high-quality operator, and we like Hecla's focus on strengthening its balance sheet.

Axogen develops products and technologies for nerve regeneration and repair. The company reported better-than-expected results for its most recent quarter. During the quarter, the Food and Drug Administration also approved a biologics license application for the company's primary product, Avance Nerve Graft. The license provides 12 years of market exclusivity in the US and paves the way for broader insurance coverage, which may increase the commercial potential of the product.

FormFactor designs and manufactures testing and measurement equipment, with an emphasis on probe cards that allow chipmakers to test products at the wafer level before they are packaged and installed in devices like cell phones. The company reported better-than-expected results for its most recent quarter as demand for FormFactor's testing products grew due to the increasing level and complexity of testing as the density and complexity of chips escalates.

Kaiser Aluminum manufactures fabricated aluminum products for aerospace and defense, beverage and food packaging, and automotive and general engineering. The company reported better-than-expected results for its most recent quarter, driven by improved pricing and sales mix in premium markets, like aerospace and packaging. We believe the company can continue to grow sales and margins as facility and product lines expansions become fully operational, serving the aerospace and coated packaging markets, respectively.

Outfront Media is an advertising company that has billboards and transit displays. The company has been converting traditional static billboard advertising to digital displays, which has brought in new advertisers and driven growth. One of the company's key contracts is with the New York Metropolitan Transit Authority (MTA). The digital displays have been generating more revenue per display because multiple advertisements are able to run in rotation with dynamic content updates, as well as increasing ridership in the MTA.

The leading detractors in the quarter were Treace Medical Concepts, Inc. (TMCI), Thryv Holdings, Inc. (THRY), Backblaze, Inc. Class A (BLZE), Janus International Group, Inc. (JBI) and Universal Technical Institute, Inc (UTI).

Treace Medical is an orthopedic medical device company focused on bunion and midfoot surgery. Shares have been pressured by share loss to less invasive procedures. With a large addressable market and Treace's own move toward less invasive systems, our investment thesis remains intact. The company is improving its operating efficiency and has a strong balance sheet.

Thryv provides a software platform for customer relationship management and digital and print marketing services for small businesses. Weakness during the quarter reflected challenges upselling legacy customers to its software services. Because Thryv remains committed to cross-selling to higher-margin services, it is applying ample cash flows from legacy activities to strengthen its balance sheet.

Backblaze provides cloud storage and computer backup solutions. After a strong first half in 2025, decelerating growth trends in cloud storage among their target customer base of AI-related startups emerged alongside changed corporate accounting practices. Given the lack of clarity for the company going forward, we exited our position.

Janus is a global manufacturer and supplier of solutions for the self-storage industry, providing doors, hallway systems, mobile storage units, automation systems and smart-entry technologies. Revenue and earnings fell short for the quarter, because some smaller clients paused orders for repair and maintenance work, but management is confident these delayed projects will ultimately resume. Janus continues to enjoy strong market share, resilient cash flow and growth potential in smart technologies.

Universal Technical Institute offers transportation and skilled-trades training programs, as well as continuing education programs in the allied health, dental, nursing, patient care and diagnostic fields. Shares traded down as expansion costs from new campuses and educational products pressured near-term profitability. UTI is positioned to benefit from anticipated ongoing strong demand for skilled labor.

We appreciate your confidence and thank you for your support.

Sincerely,

First Eagle Investments
